data.title: 11 dead, over 60 missing after Pakistan shopping centre fire smoulders for 24 hours
data.description:
Firefighters in Karachi began searching on ‌Monday for more than 60 missing people after a massive fire destroyed a shopping centre in Pakistan’s most populous city, while the death toll from the disaster rose to 11.
The fire started late on Saturday at the sprawling, multi-storey Gul Plaza in the city’s business district and ⁠burned for more than a day, hampering rescue efforts in the densely packed area.
After more than 24 hours of firefighting, crews moved to cooling and debris clearing on Sunday, although concerns grew that more victims could still be trapped inside.
Police surgeon Summaiya Syed said the death toll had risen to 11, while Karachi Mayor Murtaza Wahab said on Sunday that more than 60 people were still missing.
Firefighters said the lack of ventilation in the building, which houses more than 1,200 shops, caused thick smoke to fill the mall and slowed efforts to reach people trapped inside.

“It appears to have been caused by a circuit breaker,” Sindh province police chief Javed Alam Odho told reporters at the site on Sunday, adding that the layout of the market and the presence of flammable materials such as carpets and blankets caused parts of the fire to continue smouldering.
By Sunday evening, large sections of the building had collapsed, leaving twisted metal and debris strewn across the street, along with fallen air-conditioning units and shop signboards. Rescuers warned that the remaining structure was unstable and could collapse further.
According to rescue services, authorities received the first emergency call at 10.38pm on Saturday, reporting that ground-floor shops were on fire. By the time firefighters arrived, the flames had already spread to the upper floors, engulfing much of the building.
Shopkeepers and residents told local media that a delayed response and shortages of water and equipment had hampered early firefighting efforts, fuelling anger among traders who said decades of livelihoods had been wiped out.
Karachi, the capital of Sindh province, has a history of deadly fires, often blamed on poor safety standards and illegal construction. In November 2023, a fire at a shopping centre in the city killed 10 people and injured 22 others.
A massive fire at a garments factory in Karachi in 2012 killed 260 people.
Additional reporting by Associated Press

data.title: Chinese team restores legendary Tang dynasty ‘golden’ armour found in Tibetan tomb
data.description:
“We will not leave the desert till we beat the foe, although in war our golden armour be outworn 100 times.”
Wang Changling’s famous poem from China’s Tang dynasty depicts soldiers in golden armour battling on the desert frontiers. However, for a long time, no Tang dynasty gold-plated armour was unearthed, leaving its appearance to the imagination.
But on January 14, the Key Laboratory of Archaeological Sciences and Cultural Heritage at the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences (CASS) unveiled the only known physical example of Tang dynasty “golden” armour – a meticulously restored suit of gilded bronze armour found in a royal tomb on the Tibetan plateau.

The restoration team behind the project not only rebuilt the artefact piece by piece but also produced a video reconstruction showing the likely appearance of the original gold armour.
The artefacts were salvaged and restored from the Tuyuhun royal tombs between 2022 and 2025, including armour, lacquerware and metal objects.
“We adopted a strategy of ‘disassembling the whole into parts and reassembling the parts into a whole’, conducting layered cleaning, extraction and protection while meticulously cataloguing each armour plate,” cultural heritage conservation expert Guo Zhengchen told a press conference at the armour unveiling.
The king’s tomb had been hit by looting and salvage excavations, leaving the armour plates in poor condition.
A 2024 excavation report in the journal Archaeology, titled “Excavation of Tomb No 1 at the Hot Water Cemetery in Dulan County, Qinghai, 2018,” describes the find: “Several bronze armour plates were discovered … The plates are nearly rectangular with a semicircular lower edge, measuring 10cm long, 5cm wide, and 0.3cm thick. A large number of lacquered armour pieces were also found”.
The copper and lacquered armour fragments were found intermixed and piled, with no clear structural relationship. They were fragile, at risk of shattering at the slightest touch.
“We recorded the original spatial information of each plate via 3D scanning and analysed their manufacturing techniques and composition using scanning electron microscopy and ultra-depth microscopy,” the conservators said.
Their analysis revealed that this was not merely bronze armour but gold armour with additional gilding. Based on this finding, they created a detailed restoration video.
The reconstructed armour in the video brings to life another Tang poem: “Like golden scale on water sunrays strike our coats of mail”.
Based on gold artefacts and silk textiles in the tomb and tree-ring dating of its wooden structure, the burial was dated to the mid-8th century – nearly 1,300 years ago – the conservation report stated. It said the tomb’s occupant was confirmed to be a Tuyuhun king and the gold armour displayed was likely among his most prized possessions.

The Tuyuhun kingdom was subjugated successively by the Sui and Tang dynasties. It later became a vassal of the Tubo empire based on the Tibetan plateau. In addition to the gold armour, the tomb yielded a large quantity of equestrian equipment and iron and lacquered armour.
This aligns with records in the New Book of Tang, compiled by 11th century historian Ouyang Xiu, which said the Tubo possessed “excellent armour covering the entire body, with only openings for the eyes, so that strong bows and sharp blades could not inflict serious harm”.
Dulan county, where the tomb was discovered, was an important transit point for east-west trade. There, the Qinghai Route was a vital artery of the Silk Road, allowing trade caravans from the Tubo empire and the Tang dynasty to travel east to Changan (modern Xian) and west to Xinjiang, Persia (modern Iran) and various western regions.

data.title: Meet Neville Sarony, the Hong Kong barrister who also writes, acts and sings
data.description:
I GREW UP IN A theatrical family. My mother was also on the stage and had just begun to really make a name for herself when she married my father (Leslie Sarony). Then we saw a little bit of his true character because he said, “There’s only one star in this family,” and she was told she couldn’t work any more. We used to go to wherever my father was playing summer seasons. He was half of The Two Leslies, which was the partnership that he began in, I think, 1937. I grew up watching them on stage. My father began (in vaudeville) before the first world war. He told me he first realised he could write songs during the war. So, the upshot is, I think, nearly 600 songs of his. And my brothers and I still get the royalties, which is rather fun.
THERE ARE THREE of us, all boys, I’m the eldest. There was not much distance between my birth and the outbreak of the second world war. We were living in London. My father had just bought this house and he decided it was dangerous for us to live there. So, he moved us to Yorkshire, to Huddersfield, where we lived for the next five years. After the war, we went back to London. At the age of 13, I went to a boarding school in Kent called Sutton Valence. It was 1952, things were coming off rationing. Before I left school, I was thinking of becoming an actor (but later decided to study law).

TWO WEEKS AFTER I left school I was in the army doing National Service. I joined the Queen’s Own Royal West Kent Regiment (which existed until 1961) and I was based in Maidstone. I took the War Office Selection Board (a series of tests to identify officer material) and then I was sent to the Mons Officer Cadet School (in Aldershot in Hampshire). I was commissioned out of Mons into the 7th Gurkha Rifles and sent to (then) Malaya, for arguably the two best years of my life. I was introduced to a culture that became part of my life. I was the battalion intelligence officer. The first thing I had to do was learn the language, Gorkhali – a mix of Nepalese and Urdu – otherwise no one speaks to you. It was quite an intense learning period, but I loved it. And then some Malay. The (Malayan) Emergency (also known as the Anti-British National Liberation War) was still on and we were hunting terrorists in the jungle.
IT WAS SO TEMPTING (to stay with the Gurkhas) but I had been offered a place at LSE (the London School of Economics) to read law and I had from an early age wanted to become a barrister. In my last year at LSE I got married. My wife was Nepali. She was the daughter of a Gurkha officer in our 1st Battalion, one of the first Gurkha officers to be given the Queen’s Commission and we met at Buckingham Palace. The queen was presenting a new pipe banner to the 6th Gurkhas (6th Queen Elizabeth’s Own Gurkha Rifles) and all brigade officers were invited to attend. We went along and as the daughter of a serving officer, she was invited. We married in 1963 and I graduated in 1964. I spent two years in London; it was terrible. It was very difficult to start at the bar and solicitors were notorious for paying late. So, we went to Nepal for three years and I wrote about that in my book Counsel in the Clouds (2014). I was the first foreign lawyer to practise in Nepal. I discovered that very few people were doing corporate law and I really focused on that. I became an adviser to members of the royal family. My wife, Bimala, was very creative and she set up the first boutique where she was designing clothes based on traditional Nepali styles but giving them a sort of Western twist.

AFTER THREE YEARS we returned to London. Bim opened a boutique in St Christopher’s Place, just off Oxford Street. It was called the Third Eye Boutique. In 1971, our daughter, Tanya, was born. Bim died in 1976 and that was a terrible time. Tanya was five and we took her mother’s ashes to her home in the Himalayas. I had to somehow look after Tanya and make a living. I married one of Bim’s sisters, which was probably a mistake. She wouldn’t live in the house, so we bought an almost derelict house in Chelsea and practically rebuilt it. (They divorced in 2000).
I DECIDED I DIDN’T LIKE the way the legal profession was going in England and I was thinking about America, but out of the blue a friend called from Hong Kong saying he was setting up chambers there. So, we came to Hong Kong in 1985. Tanya went to the Swiss German International School. I like doing both criminal and civil cases, which is not very common.

THE CASE WHICH probably excited the most emotion was the Shek Kip Mei one (in 1994), where 12 people died in a fire at a branch of HSBC and (my client) was charged with all 12 murders. He was an ordinary father of two daughters and then his wife disappeared. He spent three days looking for her and then went to the bank where he believed a friend (of his wife’s) worked and demanded to know where she was. He held a can of paint thinner and a lighter and told everyone to get out of the bank. While he was standing there talking, a police officer walked in and shouted at the man, and the lighter ignited the vapour from the can, which set fire to him and to the bank. HSBC had blocked up the rear exits of the bank so the only way out was through the front, which was now on fire. (He was given a 20-year jail sentence for manslaughter.)
(LAST SUMMER) we did an inquest where the jury returned an unlawful killing verdict of the father of a family we were representing. (In 2012) a taxi driver was arrested by the police and they hauled him up by his neck, broke his spine and made him paraplegic. He died 30 days later. The first inquest was held in 2018 and the jury returned a verdict of unlawful killing. Then the officer did a judicial review of the verdict, arguing that the coroner had misdirected the jury. So, a second inquest was directed and that jury also returned a verdict of unlawful killing. It was, in some ways, the most emotional outcome to a verdict that I’ve experienced.

I HAVE WRITTEN three novels, which are all based on a character called Max Devlin, who is an ex-Gurkha officer. Another one I’m working on is a book of legal anecdotes, which (my wife), Angel (Lau Wing-yi), is editing. It’s really a series of things which happened mainly to me, sometimes to others. Actually, Angel was a journalist. She’d done a criminology degree at Edinburgh. And then she came to the bar. She was introduced to me as a pupil and then things basically developed. I suppose it’s the longest period of engagement you could think of; it took me 16 years to actually pop the question, but we eventually got around to it.
IN DECEMBER 1997, I was preparing to leave Hong Kong. I was staying at a hotel in Kowloon and I looked out of my window and I saw this sign on the back of a building – it said “traditional jazz” and it was Ned Kelly’s Last Stand in Tsim Sha Tsui (where Sarony now sings regularly, having relocated to London only briefly in 1997). In 2004, I was approached by the Faust Festival to audition for this play, The Dresser, by Ronald Harwood. It’s a super part. The whole play is built around the relationship between the dresser and the actor-manager, who is a sort of old-fashioned actor-manager taking his own touring company around, putting on Shakespeare mainly. So the manager is on stage the whole time, he never leaves. My dressing table faced out three feet from the audience and I would make up my face into a mirror that wasn’t there. It was fascinating. The director (Jessica Lefkow) was clever.

THE PIANO IS, I think, my sort of go-to place when things are bad, I can go and play them out. I get a huge amount of satisfaction from music. I think one of the greatest joys for me is singing with good musicians. And that’s so much fun. Jason Cheng runs a big IT company but he moonlights as a great jazz pianist and he and I, we work together (Sarony singing). We have a sort of symbiotic relationship. We can literally do anything.
I DIVIDE MY time between Javea, in Valencia, Spain, and Hong Kong – Hong Kong is work and in Spain I’m writing a book of legal anecdotes drawn from things that happened in my career.

data.title: China’s demographic alarms blare as births hit historic low and population shrinks again
data.description:
China’s birth count plummeted to a record low last year, falling by about 10 million from its 2016 peak and slashing the total by more than half in less than a decade, as the country’s population shrank for a fourth consecutive year.
Only 7.92 million babies were born in 2025, down 17 per cent from 9.54 million in 2024, according to data released by the National Bureau of Statistics on Monday. This marked the lowest birth figure since records began in 1949 and broke the previous record low set in 2023.
China’s total population also fell by 3.39 million in 2025 to 1.4049 billion from 1.4083 billion a year earlier, the bureau said. By sheer numbers, that marked the steepest annual population decline on record, apart from during China’s devastating famine from 1959 to 1961.
Meanwhile, about 11.31 million people died last year – one of the highest totals in five decades.
“The pace of the decline is striking, particularly in the absence of major shocks,” said Su Yue, principal economist for China at the Economist Intelligence Unit.
She added that a reluctance among young people to get married, along with rising economic pressures – particularly an increase for women in the perceived cost of stepping away from employment – served as major birth deterrents.
“The data should serve as a strong signal for policymakers to place greater emphasis on domestic structural reforms,” she noted, calling for a more forceful policy response on fertility in the face of a shrinking population and the risk of a smaller consumer base in the future.
Over the past year, China has accelerated efforts to shore up its faltering birth rate by rolling out a series of family-support policies aimed at lowering child-rearing costs and easing the pressures that have deterred couples from having children.
Most notably, Beijing launched a national childcare subsidy scheme offering up to 10,800 yuan (US$1,534) per year for each child under three – the most far-reaching pronatalist measure since China allowed families to have three children in 2021.
In the early stages of population decline, fluctuations [in births] are common before the trend stabilises
Yuan Xin, China Population Association
Yuan Xin, vice-president of the China Population Association and a professor of demography at Nankai University in Tianjin, pointed to a correlation between the decline in births last year and the sharp drop in marriages in 2024, noting that marriage registrations typically serve as a leading indicator of birth trends in the following year.
China’s mainland registered 6.106 million marriages in 2024, down more than 20 per cent year on year, marking the lowest level since 1980. However, through the first three quarters of 2025, China recorded a year-on-year uptick of 8.5 per cent in marriage registrations, with analysts signalling that a host of pro-marriage policies were starting to pay off.
And while the nation’s full-year marriage figures have yet to be released, several major municipalities have reported sharp increases in registrations. Shanghai recorded a 38.7 per cent year-on-year rise in marriages in 2025, while Fujian province said the number of newly registered couples grew by 12 per cent.
Yuan, with the China Population Association, projected that the total number of marriages reached around 6.9 million last year, and he expected this to push births in 2026 to above 8 million.
“In the early stages of population decline, fluctuations [in births] are common before the trend stabilises,” he said. “But despite short-term volatility, a return to positive growth is almost off the table.”
Late last year, central authorities also pledged to expand insurance coverage to cover all childbirth-related out-of-pocket expenses, and they have sought to regulate childcare by addressing long-standing shortages, uneven quality and weak oversight in the sector.
At the same time, the government has moved to make it easier to register for marriage and harder to get a divorce, as shrinking marriage rates delay family formation and compound population ageing.
As the number of women of childbearing age shrinks, fertility intentions remain weak, and marriage and childbirth continue to be delayed, much stronger support is required at both national and local levels, according to experts.

data.title: Hackers target Iran state TV with exiled prince’s message to forces
data.description:
Hackers disrupted Iranian state television satellite transmissions to air footage supporting the country’s exiled crown prince and calling on security forces to not “point your weapons at the people”, footage online showed early Monday, the latest disruption to follow nationwide protests in the country.
The hacking came as the death toll in a crackdown by authorities that smothered the demonstrations reached at least 3,919 people killed, activists said. They fear the number will grow far higher as information leaks out of a country still gripped by the government’s decision to shut down the internet.
Meanwhile, tensions remain high between the United States and Iran over the crackdown after US President Donald Trump drew two red lines for the Islamic Republic - the killing of peaceful protesters and Tehran conducting mass executions in the wake of the demonstrations.
The USS Abraham Lincoln aircraft carrier, which days earlier had been in the South China Sea, passed Singapore overnight to enter the Strait of Malacca - putting it on a route that could bring it to the Middle East. Tracking data showed that the USS Frank E. Petersen Jr., the USS Michael Murphy and the USS Spruance, all Arleigh Burke-class guided missile destroyers, were travelling with the Lincoln through the strait.

The footage aired Sunday night across multiple channels broadcast by satellite from Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting, the country’s state broadcaster which has a monopoly on television and radio broadcasting.
The video aired two clips of exiled Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi, then included footage of security forces and others in what appeared to be Iranian police uniforms. It claimed without offering evidence others had “laid down their weapons and swore an oath of allegiance to the people”.
“This is a message to the army and security forces,” one graphic read. “Don’t point your weapons at the people. Join the nation for the freedom of Iran.”
The semi-official Fars news agency, believed to be close to the country’s paramilitary Revolutionary Guard, quoted a statement from the state broadcaster acknowledging that the signal in “some areas of the country was momentarily disrupted by an unknown source”. It did not discuss what had been aired.
A statement from Pahlavi’s office acknowledged the disruption that showed the crown prince. It did not respond to questions from Associated Press about the hack.
“I have a special message for the military. You are the national army of Iran, not the Islamic Republic army,” Pahlavi said in the hacked broadcast. “You have a duty to protect your own lives. You don’t have much time left. Join the people as soon as possible.”

Social media footage shared abroad, possibly from those with Starlink satellites to get around the internet shutdown, showed the hack in progress across multiple channels. Pahlavi’s campaign also shared the footage.
Sunday’s hack is not the first to see Iranian airwaves disrupted. In 1986, The Washington Post reported that the CIA supplied the prince’s allies “a miniaturised television transmitter for an 11-minute clandestine broadcast” to Iran by Pahlavi that pirated the signal of two stations in the Islamic Republic.
In 2022, multiple channels aired footage showing leaders from the exiled opposition group Mujahedeen-e-Khalq and a graphic calling for the death of Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
Pahlavi’s father, Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, fled Iran ahead of the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Pahlavi, the son, urged protesters onto the streets on January 8 as Iranian authorities shut down the internet and drastically intensified their crackdown.
How much support Pahlavi has inside Iran remains an open question, though there have been pro-shah cries at the demonstrations.
The death toll exceeds that of any other round of protest or unrest in Iran in decades, and recalls the chaos surrounding the 1979 revolution. The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency put the death toll Sunday to at least 3,919 people killed, warning it likely would go higher.
Iranian officials have not given a clear death toll, although on Saturday, Khamenei said the protests had left “several thousand” people dead and blamed the United States for the deaths. It was the first indication from an Iranian leader of the extent of the casualties from the wave of protests that began December 28 over Iran’s ailing economy.

data.title: What an ancient Chinese hero’s fatal love of raw fish warns us about uncooked food
data.description:
Tokyo’s famed New Year fish auction has smashed its own headline record, with a giant bluefin tuna fetching an eye-watering price. At the Toyosu fish market’s first auction of 2026, a 243kg (535lb) bluefin tuna was snapped up for 510 million yen (US$3.2 million), making it the most expensive tuna ever sold at this annual ritual.
The winning bid came from Kiyomura, the company behind the popular Sushi Zanmai chain. The purchase eclipsed the company’s own record-setting bid from 2019.
The fish itself was caught off Oma in northern Japan, a region famed for producing premium sushi-grade tuna that reliably sends bidders into a frenzy. As always, the pre-dawn spectacle drew crowds and cameras.
Beyond bragging rights for Kiyomura, this year’s splurge reflects a cautiously optimistic trend: bluefin stocks are slowly recovering, thanks to conservation measures after years of overfishing. That has made these record-breaking sales feel a little less controversial than they once did.

Raw seafood is – or used to be – one of my favourite foods. I have long fancied myself something of a connoisseur of Japanese sashimi and sushi, preferring the lighter, subtler flavours of lean tuna, sea bream and squid to the bolder, oilier notes of mackerel and salmon, the latter being my least favourite fish when eaten raw.
I get triggered when I see diners drowning slices of sashimi in soy sauce, or worse, stirring wasabi-in-a-tube – usually horseradish with food colouring and assorted additives – into the soy sauce to produce a disgusting green sludge that bears an unfortunate resemblance to the pea soup chucked up by the demon-possessed girl in The Exorcist.
In the past year or so, however, I have drastically cut back on uncooked seafood and meat. It is not just sashimi and sushi that have fallen by the wayside, but also former indulgences such as raw oysters on the half shell, steak tartare and Korean yukhoe. I no longer dare order a medium-rare hamburger, and I will only eat salads that I have prepared myself.
This retreat has nothing to do with woo-woo notions about raw food having bad qi that causes stomach upsets, an article of faith for my mother and many others of her generation. It has everything to do with a growing reluctance to ingest parasites and pathogens that are not neutralised by heat.
Food safety authorities have warned for years that uncooked seafood and meat are high-risk foods, no matter how fresh, expensive or expertly prepared. Most of us, myself included, simply chose to ignore them.

I pay heed now, probably because of the cowardice and instinct for self-preservation that come with age, coupled with the dawning realisation that my five-decade-old immune system is no longer what it once was.
History offers a sobering cautionary tale. Chen Deng (163-201), celebrated for his heroism, loyalty and learning, died because of his love for raw fish.
An able district administrator who improved the lives of the people under his charge, Chen was also a capable military commander.
He fell gravely ill one day, and the famed physician Hua Tuo (140-208) correctly diagnosed a parasitic infection due to the over-consumption of raw fish. He prescribed a herbal concoction that caused Chen to throw up three sheng – about 600 millilitres, or 20 fluid ounces – of red, wriggling worms. It is an image worth recalling the next time one is tempted to eat something uncooked.
Chen, alas, died not long after, at the tender age of 38.
These days, my appetite for raw delicacies is tempered by health concerns and the vivid mental picture of Hua Tuo’s unfortunate patient. Some delicacies, I have decided, are simply not worth the gut ache. Or worse.

data.title: Philippines enters its ‘hour of danger’ as Marcos, Duterte clash
data.description:
For the Philippines, 2026 promises to be a roller-coaster ride of political intrigue as President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr and his deputy lock horns in an existential clash that risks tanking the economy, dividing military loyalties and derailing Asean’s regional diplomacy.
“It will be a very messy year both in politics and the economy,” Philippine Congressman Edgar Erice told This Week in Asia.
A showdown now seems all but inevitable: Vice-President Sara Duterte enters 2026 with an approval rating of 56 per cent and a trust rating of 54 per cent, according to December polling – a commanding 20-point lead over Marcos, whose ratings languish at 34 and 32 per cent, respectively.
Analysts say those numbers translate into a plausible pathway to the presidency for Duterte at the next election in 2028, a pathway that Marcos appears determined to block.
It is their “ora de peligro”, according to economic risk analyst Jonathan Ravelas – distilling the political peril into a common Filipino expression, derived from Spanish, that translates to “hour of danger”.

The two camps are preparing for mutually assured destruction. Both face threats ranging from impeachment complaints and criminal prosecutions to military intervention, set against a backdrop of the economy’s weakening fundamentals, shifting loyalty networks and unforeseen natural disasters.
Duterte is widely expected to run for president in 2028 while Marcos, constitutionally barred from seeking another term, is likely to hand-pick a surrogate candidate to protect him from payback, according to political analyst Ronald Llamas.
The immediate battleground centres on Duterte’s handling of confidential funds. Last month, the ombudsman’s office filed a plunder case against her over 612.5 million pesos (US$10.3 million) in funds that her office expended in just 11 days in 2022.
An impeachment complaint is expected next month, according to Llamas, though he expressed scepticism about its success.
For Duterte’s critics, the real prize lies in exposing her personal bank accounts. Former senator Antonio Trillanes IV has alleged these accounts contain transactions “from anomalous, questionable sources” – including casino barons to drug lords – assertions that Llamas believes could prove politically devastating if brought to light.

Doing so would require the ombudsman to file an impeachment complaint with the House of Representatives, attaching detailed bank transaction records. Legal experts maintain that the ombudsman is granted this power by both the law that established the office and the Bank Secrecy Act, which allows officials’ private accounts to be examined “in cases of impeachment”.
But any such effort is likely to be escalated to the Supreme Court and already faces opposition from congressmen, including Erice who questioned whether banks would actually comply without a court order.
Marcos, meanwhile, faces a possible impeachment complaint of his own over the billions of pesos spent that were on anomalous flood control projects the president himself approved in national budgets from 2023 to 2025.
Erice, who said he would not endorse either impeachment attempt, nonetheless described flood control as a bigger scandal than Duterte’s questionable use of confidential intelligence funds.

“I don’t think that the executive doesn’t know this,” he said. “They allowed these [diversions of flood control funds to] substandard projects. So they are equally liable.”
Erice predicts his fellow lawmakers will delay a decision on impeaching Duterte, particularly as the president’s popularity crumbles while hers climbs. An attempt was already made to impeach her last year.
Llamas, meanwhile, struck a more ominous note. The former presidential adviser said he did not expect Marcos to be unseated because “the Americans don’t want Duterte, who is pro-China, installed”.
“Unless an assassination occurs,” he said. “If that happens, Sara will succeed him.”
Coup fears
That grim prospect took on a troubling new urgency earlier this month when a Philippine Army colonel publicly announced via social media his withdrawal of support from Marcos, who as president is also the commander in chief.
“It’s too much, enough already”, Audie Mongao wrote, without elaborating. The soon-to-retire colonel, who was on leave at the time, has since returned to his unit to face potential legal charges.
It followed the arrest on January 5 of Romeo Poquiz, a former air force general who served under former president Rodrigo Duterte, father of the current vice-president. Poquiz was charged with sedition after urging the military to withdraw its support for Marcos.
Trillanes, himself a former navy officer who withdrew his support from a former president in a failed bid to topple her, predicted “many attempts” to persuade military officers to withdraw their backing throughout 2026.

“We still do not know how sizeable the support is,” he said last month. “We will only know the extent of the threat of withdrawal of support or mass protest when it actually happens.”
Duterte loyalists, sensing weakness, have reportedly sought to spread discontent by highlighting Marcos’ failure to hold senior officials accountable for the massive flood control fraud.
A retired military chief now closely aligned with the Duterte camp was overheard boasting that “it’s just a question of time” before more officers withdraw their support, according to a businessman with knowledge of the discussions who requested anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter.
A military official confirmed to This Week in Asia that investigators were now assessing whether Mongao, who wielded influence as part of the army’s training structure, represented a growing lack of allegiance among the armed forces or was an isolated case.

Economic storm
The Philippines’ political showdown is playing out amid economic pressures that threaten to intensify voters’ grievances.
Ravelas, who is managing director of eManagement for Business and Marketing Services, expects moderate growth of 5.6 per cent in 2026.
This would mark a downward revision from the 6 to 7 per cent over the next three years that the government’s Development Budget Coordinating Committee had projected in June 2025.
“Sectors like retail, construction, real estate and manufacturing are slowing, so joblessness could rise,” Ravelas warned. “Businesses need to tighten costs, hedge currency risks and diversify markets now.”

Thousands of jobs have already been shed in the construction sector, while retail sales declined even during the recent holiday season, a bellwether for consumer demand.
Meanwhile, the Philippine peso has been depreciating to levels not seen for decades amid a strong US dollar and widening trade deficits.
Ravelas projects the exchange rate could deteriorate to 61 pesos per US dollar this year – breaching a psychological threshold of 60 pesos unheard of since World War II.
The central bank is expected to reduce its policy rate to 4 per cent this year, which should support credit growth, theoretically. But the peso’s weakness could offset any benefits, making imports costlier and debt service more onerous.
“Expect higher fuel costs and pricier debt repayments, which will squeeze budgets and push inflation up,” Ravelas said.
We are in the ‘ora de peligro’ but the president seems to be just kicking the can down the road
Jonathan Ravelas, economic risk analyst
Amid this maelstrom, Marcos has assumed chairmanship of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations – a position that demands sophisticated diplomacy at a moment when he is consumed by domestic conflicts.
“He is really focused, almost 100 per cent, on his domestic war with the Dutertes,” said sociologist Walden Bello, co-chair of the Focus on the Global South NGO in Bangkok.
Still, Marcos did announce in November that finalising a binding South China Sea Code of Conduct with China would be his “No 1 priority” as Asean chair.
His Foreign Affairs Secretary Theresa Lazaro has also previously declared that 2026 will be the year that a code finally comes to fruition.
She is scheduled to meet her Asean counterparts later this month, reportedly to discuss “a new strategy” for the code as well as her recent talks with military and civilian leaders in Myanmar.


China has also signalled openness. Last July, the state-run Global Times tabloid quoted Foreign Minister Wang Yi as saying that all parties had agreed to strive to finalise the code this year “as scheduled”.
Yet not all analysts are convinced. Dr Hunter Marston, a specialist in great power competition in Southeast Asia with the Centre for Strategic and International Studies, told This Week in Asia in November that the odds of concluding a code by 2026 “have decreased significantly under Marcos”, given his pivot back to the US.
Meanwhile, public frustration is mounting. Within the business community, calls are growing louder for the president to immediately arrest and prosecute key officials implicated in the flood control scandal.
“This is Marcos’ defining moment,” Ravelas told This Week in Asia.
“Their feeling is that time is running out,” he said. “We are in the ora de peligro but the president seems to be just kicking the can down the road and allowing all of these things to happen.”
Llamas concurred darkly. “If no big fish is charged by the ombudsman this first quarter, the situation is going to explode into political unrest,” he said.

data.title: Wealth, luck, health: racehorse owners get creative choosing lucky names in English and Chinese
data.description:
Seeing a racehorse with a name that features witty wordplay or a link to pop culture is a common sight at racetracks in Hong Kong. And for some punters, the witty name is what stands out and the reason they gamble on a particular horse.
But naming a horse is not as easy as it sounds. Breeders and owners in the city have to choose two names: one in English and one in Chinese.
Many choose auspicious names, picking words for wealth, luck or health. Others go for humour, pop culture or even food and money references. Superstition and fortune-telling also play a big role in picking lucky names.
A clever name, however, is no guarantee of success. K.L. Cheng, who once handled racehorse name approvals and is now head of dual-site stables operations at the Hong Kong Jockey Club, said horses with strange or hard-to-read names “are rarely known, rarely have good results”.
To celebrate the Year of the Horse, the Post has compiled a list of memorable horse names in English and Chinese and their meanings.
Sir Charge (Gaau Hok Fai)

Anyone who has lost a bet at the track will appreciate the name Sir Charge, a play on the word “surcharge” – the extra fee often found on receipts.
For local punters, the Chinese name Gaau Hok Fai or “paying tuition” is a familiar phrase used to describe losing money as a learning experience, whether in the stock market or at the track.
Naming the horse Gaau Hok Fai is a form of racing fan irony. It acknowledges that owning and betting on horses is an expensive education, and losing to this runner means you have “paid your tuition” for the day.
Go Go Go (Soeng Si Mei Lik)
“Go go go!” is a simple, high-octane cheer that sounds great when shouted from the grandstands as the field turns for home.
But the Chinese name Soeng Si Mei Lik means “IPO attraction”. “Soeng Si” refers to a company going public.
Hong Kong is a city with a strong financial spirit, and many local entrepreneurs dream of listing their companies on the Hong Kong stock exchange.
In a city where stock market fever is part of daily life, calling a horse “IPO attraction” suggests it is a hot asset.
The owner is treating the horse not just as an animal, but as a hot stock, which sounds like telling the public, “Invest in this horse; it will perform like a successful IPO.”
Noisy Boy (Jau Coi)

This name shows a strong contrast between images: a disciplined elite and a loud, unruly athlete.
Noisy Boy suggests a horse with personality – maybe not easy to manage in the stable or one that stands out on the track.
The Chinese name Jau Coi means “outstanding talent” or “elite”. In Hong Kong, it also refers to the government’s Quality Migrant Admission Scheme, which targets highly skilled people to boost the city’s competitiveness.
The contrast is clear. Elite usually means grace and composure, but racehorses are often “noisy boys” – energetic, raw, and sometimes rebellious.
Everstar (Ceoi San)
The English name is a classic and safe horse name, symbolising longevity and brilliance.
But the Chinese name Ceoi San means “god of cooking” or “chef god”, most likely referencing the Stephen Chow Sing-chi hit film The God of Cookery from 1996.
Why name a horse chef?
In racing slang, “cooked” means the race is over. But if a horse is called chef, it means it controls the race, beating others like a master chef.
The name is a nod to local culture and Hong Kong’s love of food.
Everyday Lettuce (Sang Coi)

No list of unusual names is complete without the retired legend Sang Coi. Though the horse has left the track, its name remains a gold standard for Cantonese wordplay.
To English speakers, it sounds like a strange diet or a very healthy meal.
The Chinese name Sang Coi is a homophone for “生财,” which means “to make money”, and is pronounced almost the same in Cantonese (Sang Coi) and Mandarin (Sheng Cai), just with a different tone.
That is why lettuce is essential at Lunar New Year. And the “Everyday” in the name, it is not about salad, but wishing for daily wealth.
It is a textbook feng shui name: a literal prayer for prosperity, hidden in the produce aisle. When a commentator calls the name, it sounds like a spell for the owner’s bank account.
What are the rules for naming a horse?
According to Hong Kong Jockey Club rules, every new horse must register both English and Chinese names within one month of arrival.
The rules are strict. No more than 18 English letters or four Chinese characters, nothing vulgar or purely for advertising, no names too similar to existing ones, and certain symbols and numbers are banned.
Like in other jurisdictions, famous horses’ names are protected for years after retirement or death. But within these limits, owners can get creative.
There is also interplay between English and Chinese. Some owners translate directly, others create a totally different “other” name. This can lead to funny or confusing combinations, especially with idioms or puns.
Sometimes, a name has deep personal meaning. Dr Gene Tsoi Wai-wang once named his endurance horse Mr Gnocchi, after his family’s golden retriever, itself named after the Italian pasta-like dumplings.

“I dared not tell anyone before he had won a race that he was named after a dog,” Tsoi said.
To engage local fans, the Hong Kong Jockey Club also gives overseas horses and jockeys a Chinese name during simulcasts.
From auspicious horse-themed phrases and couplets to whether your luck is in, check out our Year of the Horse 2026 series to discover all you need to know about the coming Lunar New Year.

data.title: Ardy Ferguson’s road to the S.Pellegrino Young Chef crown
data.description:
Ardy Ferguson seems relaxed, or as relaxed as he could be for somebody about to compete in the most prestigious young chefs’ competition in the world. The first time I meet him is in the spotless kitchen of one-Michelin-starred Belon – one of Hong Kong’s most recognisable French restaurants – where he can be found most days, more so in the immediate run-up to the grand finale of the S.Pellegrino Young Chefs Academy Competition (SPYCA).
At the age of 30, the Indonesian-Canadian has already risen the ranks to become Belon’s sous chef. He is mild-mannered but cheerful, giving the impression that he’s well-liked by everyone he meets. All the better then, as he is due in a few days to depart for Milan, Italy, where the biennial competition is to be held.
First launched in 2015 to identify the most promising young chefs worldwide, the SPYCA makes its first selection of aspiring culinarians in a regional round the first year, before moving onto the global round in the second. In 2024, Ferguson beat other talented chefs hailing from the Maldives, Thailand, Singapore, Indonesia, South Korea and Japan to clinch the Asia title. His entry, a deceptively simple creation titled “Archipelago Celebration”, took after nasi tempong, an Indonesian dish traditionally eaten during large gatherings and celebrations. Ferguson’s interpretation features Cantonese-style roast duck as the main protein, with seven other components including rice, coconut gravy and sambal. It’s a masterful balance of his classical French training with his Indonesian roots – and a dash of Cantonese soul from the city he calls home.

Ferguson’s journey into the kitchen was unexpected, even to himself. Growing up between Jakarta and Canada, he initially planned on becoming an engineer, enrolling himself in one of the most prestigious electrical engineering programmes in Canada, at the University of British Columbia. “I realised very quickly it wasn’t for me,” he says.
He left the programme and took a gap year, working at a bakery while he considered his next move. To his surprise, he enjoyed it more than he expected and decided to test it out as a career by staging (taking an unpaid internship) at various fine-dining restaurants in downtown Vancouver. After a stint at culinary school, Ferguson came across a job posting at Belon, submitted his application and the rest is history.
Though Belon’s remit is modern French cuisine, Ferguson frequently delves into his Indonesian background for personal inspiration: “That’s the food I love to eat, so that’s the food I love to cook. I’ve always wanted to explore Indonesian cuisine in a fine-dining setting.”
Little wonder, then, that soon after Ferguson was selected to represent Asia in the regionals, he was paired with his mentor, Vicky Lau, the founder and head chef of Hong Kong’s two-Michelin-starred Tate Dining Room. Renowned for mixing Chinese cuisine with French techniques, Lau has been the ideal drill sergeant for Ferguson, not only in terms of style, but discipline, too.

For several months before the competition day in late October, Lau and Ferguson drilled practice runs perfecting Archipelago Celebration from 6am to 11am, at which time Ferguson would begin to prep for service. The gruelling schedule was designed to ensure that Ferguson would complete his dish within the competition’s allotted five-hour time slot.
It seems to have done its job: Ferguson doesn’t look fazed when I ask him how he’s feeling about the days ahead, answering with the rationale of an athlete who has put in countless hours of practice before a big game: “We have a plan, we’re pretty organised, and once we’re set up, then it should be just as simple as doing it [at Belon] at 6am.”
Lau also lent an eye to the plating of Ferguson’s winning dish, along with the custom trays and knives he had commissioned from Balinese craftsmen. These included twin brothers Fuad and Shiddiq Machmud of Duē Hatuē – “they used to be tattoo artists,” he explains – and Pande Kadek Nova Diatmika of Nova Custom Knives. Ferguson was drawn to the artisans’ unconventional backgrounds; their mixture of traditional craftsmanship and art perhaps an echo of Ferguson’s own sensibilities as a chef mixing traditional food with the expressionism of fine dining.

“He even went all the way to Bali to meet with the woodworkers,” adds Lau, with obvious pride.
The effect on his finished dish is apparent. Thick wooden platters the colour of dark honey, carved in ornate, sweeping forms of flora frame the food.
It’s 6.20am on the day of the competition and the event hall in Milan’s ornate Castello Sforzesco is buzzing with excitement as Ferguson takes to the stage. He’s the second of 15 participants to present to a judging panel, comprising seven of the best chefs in the world. Many would see “going first” as a tactical disadvantage, but Ferguson, ever the optimist, rattles off the silver linings with aplomb: an early start means his jet lag would come in handy; also, the stress would all be over with quickly and he can spend the rest of the competition in relative ease.

“I really pushed Ardy to think carefully about sourcing, especially for the duck, which was a key element of the dish,” Lau tells me, shedding light on the meticulousness of the mentor-mentee duo. “He managed to bring in several specific ingredients – and even packed three [pieces of] luggage with small tools to ensure everything would run smoothly.”
On the competition floor, the kitchens are arranged in a semicircle, divided by stainless-steel countertops, allowing observers to view the bustling pockets of industry side by side. Each kitchen has a distinct rhythm: their occupants furiously slicing, stirring, blending.
At Ferguson’s station, Lau’s eyes are glued to an iPad with 13 timers all counting down, her brow furrowed, arms crossed, feet planted. As mentors aren’t allowed to assist in the cooking process, she has to content herself with watching the clock and doing taste tests.

“There was one point during the contest that she was like, ‘I wish I could touch things. I hate that I can only taste,’” Ferguson would recall afterward, laughing. “She was basically the eyes at the back of my head; she saw everything I couldn’t.”
“The most challenging part for me was stepping back,” Lau later admits. “It was frustrating at times, but also rewarding to watch Ardy handle everything with focus and adaptability under pressure. Ardy is super cool-headed in the kitchen.”
With their mentors barred from helping during the competition, each chef is assigned two assistant chefs from San Pellegrino’s partner culinary school, ALMA The School of Italian Culinary Arts, to help with whatever tasks are needed. Despite the extra hands at his disposal, Ferguson does much of the work himself. He opens an oven and plumes of dark smoke rush out. Then he looks over his shoulder at Lau and gives her a wide grin – all is going according to plan.
T-minus 35 minutes. Ferguson continues apace, moving according to the beats of a happy-go-lucky soundtrack playing in his head, but the tempo is picking up, hinting at a looming sense of urgency. Bowls and pans litter the countertop, crowding the previously clutter-free operation. Almost-finished components of the dish are lined up in tidy rows. The iPad, with its various ticking timers, has been tucked away.

Ferguson is still smiling as he slices away at a large hunk of duck meat while a restless Lau watches on, her hands having moved from a leisurely clasp behind her back to being planted squarely on her hips.
T-minus 15 minutes. The calm has finally broken. Ferguson and his two chefs are plating at full speed – with seven components to the dish, it is no small feat. Ten minutes: the wooden trays remain empty. He’s now slicing the duck into precise segments. Five minutes: Ferguson is still stirring something in a pot. I’m not the only one beginning to question his timing at this point. With one minute left on the board, he’s (quite literally) doing last-minute plating. Somehow he’s managing to look more relaxed than Lau, whose hands are now in a vice-like cross in front of her body, as if she’s afraid they’ll break loose of their own accord to help. The final few seconds have the drama of a cooking reality show, as Ferguson puts his finishing touches on the trays and the clock finally counts down to zero.
Having a slew of internationally renowned chefs – like Jeremy Chan, who heads two-Michelin-starred Ikoyi in London and Julien Royer, who helms the three-Michelin-starred Odette in Singapore (along with Hong Kong’s own Louise) – taste your food might be a nerve-racking experience, but for Ferguson, it’s mostly pride – and relief.

“These are chefs I have looked up to for many years. Chef Jeremy Chan has his signature rice dish. And he’s asking me how to cook rice our way,” Ferguson throws up his hands, still in disbelief. “It’s like, OK! I’ll tell you.” A proud smile. “There are no secrets.”
I watch as Chan takes his first bite and his face lights up. “The moment I smelled it, it took me back to my childhood,” Chan says, the warmth in his voice palpable. High praise, and perhaps one of the best things that can be said about food: its ability to connect us to home, no matter where we happen to be in that moment.
As the time comes for Ferguson, North Europe finalist Noah Wynants and French finalist Antonis Avouri to take the stage as the top three contestants, it is all the trio can do to contain their nerves. “The three of us were just shaking, waiting. The anticipation,” says Ferguson. “But there’s also just the sense of a weight being lifted, you know. Just a sense of happiness that all the hard work over the past two years finally paid off.”
And pay off it did. With a final flourish, Ferguson’s name is announced as the winner and the room suddenly fills with confetti. Cheers erupt as Ferguson is hoisted onto his fellow contestants’ shoulders, and friends and family rush to the stage.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Sanpellegrino Official (@sanpellegrino_official)



One could say Lau is almost as happy as Ferguson: “I was so excited for Ardy – I felt like it was the start of a whole new chapter for him,” Lau recalls of the moment. “He’s super talented whilst also being very humble.”
For Ferguson, it was a way of telling his parents: “‘I know it was rough at first, but I get to showcase our cuisine on an international stage. And to thank them for always supporting me.”
Seems like it was worth it – after all, cooking is engineering you can eat.

data.title: Man found guilty of involuntary manslaughter of San Francisco Thai grandfather
data.description:
A man has been found guilty of involuntary manslaughter in the death of an older Thai man whose 2021 killing in San Francisco helped spark a national movement against anti-Asian-American violence.
A jury did not find Antoine Watson, 24, guilty of murder when it returned a verdict on Thursday for the January 2021 attack on Vicha Ratanapakdee, 84. Jurors found Watson guilty on the lesser charges of involuntary manslaughter and assault.
The office of San Francisco District Attorney Brooke Jenkins declined to comment, saying that the jury was still empanelled. Jurors will return on January 26 to hear arguments on aggravating factors, and sentencing will be scheduled once that is completed, the office said in an email.
Vicha Ratanapakdee was out for his usual morning walk in the quiet neighbourhood he lived in with his wife, daughter and her family when Watson charged at him and knocked him to the ground. The encounter was captured on a neighbour’s security camera. Ratanapakdee never regained consciousness and died two days later.
His family says he was attacked because of his race, but hate crime charges were not filed and the argument was not raised in trial. Prosecutors have said hate crimes are difficult to prove absent statements by the suspect.

Watson testified on the stand that he was in a haze of confusion and anger at the time of the unprovoked attack, according to KRON-TV. He said he lashed out and did not know that Ratanapakdee was Asian or older.
San Francisco Public Defender Mano Raju, whose office defended Watson, extended his sympathies to the victim’s family and said the defendant was “fully remorseful for his mistake”.
“While this death was a terrible tragedy and has garnered a lot of press attention, the importance of our legal system is that it gives us a chance to look at the facts in a balanced way,” he said in a statement.
Hundreds of people in five other US cities joined in commemorating the anniversary of Ratanapakdee’s death in 2022, seeking justice for Asian-Americans who have been harassed, assaulted and even killed in alarming numbers since the start of the Covid-19 pandemic.
Asians in America have long been subject to prejudice and discrimination, but the attacks escalated sharply after Covid-19 first appeared in late 2019 in Wuhan, China.
More than 10,000 hate incidents against Asian-Americans and Pacific Islanders were reported to the Stop AAPI Hate coalition from March 2020 through September 2021.
The attacks involved shunning, racist taunting and physical assaults.
